notes: Login flow: GET /login renders a form POSTing to /session with fields 'identifier' and 'secret'. Credentials admin@example.com/password and user@example.com/password both authenticated successfully. Session token is a static string 'ok', not a secure random token.

observations.value: POST /session with identifier=admin@example.com and secret=password returns HTTP 200 with Set-Cookie: session=ok and a 'Welcome.' home page, indicating successful login.
POST /session with identifier=user@example.com and secret=password also returns HTTP 200 with Set-Cookie: session=ok and a 'Welcome.' home page, indicating successful login.
The session cookie issued is a static, non-random value 'session=ok; Path=/', meaning any user (or attacker) can forge a valid session by setting Cookie: session=ok without ever authenticating.
The /session endpoint appears to accept any submitted credentials (both admin@example.com and user@example.com with 'password' succeed), suggesting no real credential validation is being performed.
